<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims.qmd@3a0b64e5b
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Spinoza Verification and Proof-Carrying Claims

Claim ledgers remember what the system believes. Spinoza asks what has to
happen before one of those beliefs is allowed to become stronger.

The answer is not "make every sentence a theorem." Most claims in an AI
system will not fit cleanly into a proof assistant. Some need citations. Some
need replay logs. Some need executable procedures. Some need benchmark
artifacts. Some need human or adversarial review because the problem is not
only formal validity but interpretation, stakes, and scope. Spinoza is the
layer that chooses the right kind of justification and refuses to treat an
impressive-looking artifact as broader evidence than it really is.

The reader throughline is simple: a proof artifact earns trust by narrowing
the claim it touches.

That sentence matters because proof language is dangerous when it becomes
rhetorical. A theorem tag, citation bundle, verifier log, or procedure trace
can make a weak claim look finished. Spinoza should have the opposite effect.
It should make the exact claim smaller, more inspectable, easier to challenge,
and harder to launder into a stronger support state.

## Problem

Generated prose can sound justified before it is justified.

A model can propose a claim, a proof sketch, a citation, a code path, a replay
story, or a benchmark interpretation. None of those proposals is the same
thing as verification. The system still has to ask what the claim means, what
tier of support it is seeking, what artifact would count, who or what checked
the artifact, what failed, and what the result is allowed to change.

Claim ledgers give the claim an identity. Spinoza gives the claim an
inspection envelope. A claim enters the envelope with a requested support
tier, an interpretation mapping, a candidate justification artifact, and a
consumer context. It leaves with a scoped update, no update, downgrade, block,
or escalation. Passing the verifier is not the only meaningful outcome.
Failure, timeout, and mismatch are evidence too.

This is why the chapter belongs after claim ledgers. A ledger without
verification can become bookkeeping around unsupported belief. Verification
without a ledger can produce isolated artifacts that no later reader knows how
to use. Spinoza connects the two.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Neural generation is not a verifier.

It can produce a formal-looking object that proves the wrong target. It can
cite a source that is adjacent but not supportive. It can write a convincing
procedure without recording the execution environment. It can state that a
proof failed and then continue in a tone that implies success. These failures
are not accidents outside the design envelope. They are the reason the
envelope exists.

External formal-methods work gives the right comparison point. Proof-Carrying
Code pairs executable artifacts with machine-checkable evidence against a
host policy. Lean gives the proof-assistant vocabulary for propositions,
records, inductive types, tactics, and proof terms. Those references ground
the book's proof-carrying language, but they do not make this repository a
Proof-Carrying Code implementation, and they do not make the current Lean
modules semantically complete.

The hard boundary is interpretation. A proof can be valid and still not mean
what the prose claim meant. A source can be real and still not support the
reading attached to it. A replay log can be faithful and still depend on
environment assumptions. Spinoza therefore treats interpretation mapping,
artifact validity, semantic adequacy, and support-state effect as separate
fields.

## Core Claim

Spinoza-style verification should convert high-value claims into
proof-carrying or justification-carrying artifacts with explicit tiers. This
remains an architectural argument.

The source basis is bounded. Spinoza supplies the core discipline: neural
systems may propose, but symbolic or structured verifiers decide; formal scope
is explicit; outward claims carry tiers; failed, timed-out, or unknown results
do not become success. GenesisCode supplies the broader obligation-envelope
pattern for executable artifacts: tests, property checks, proofs, provenance,
resource budgets, capability policies, replay logs, and small trusted-core
boundaries. Verification Bandwidth explains why fluent generation and large
context do not substitute for joint verification. TreeLLM supplies a possible
semantic-path substrate for interpretation mappings. Coherence Exchange is
useful verification-supply-chain framing, but it remains speculative
connector/source-note context.

The live chapter core claim stays at `argument` support. The repository has a
proof-carrying-claim schema, synthetic fixtures, a local harness, and finite
Lean predicates over record fields. It does not have an open-domain verifier,
proof generator, citation validator, semantic-equivalence checker,
source-interpretation review, theorem-validity result, runtime trace, or
whole-system epistemic-correctness result.

## Mechanism

Spinoza wraps selected claims in a justification envelope.

The envelope begins with the claim ledger. The claim has an identity, current
support state, source references, and requested tier. The verifier then needs
an interpretation mapping: the bridge between the prose claim and the object
being checked. Only after that mapping exists does the system choose a
justification type.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Claim ledger record"] --> B["Requested support tier"]
  B --> C["Interpretation mapping"]
  C --> D["Justification type"]
  D --> E["Proof, citation, procedure, replay, benchmark, or tribunal dossier"]
  E --> F{"Verifier result"}
  F -- "pass" --> G["Scoped update"]
  F -- "fail" --> H["Downgrade or block"]
  F -- "timeout or mismatch" --> I["Residual or tribunal route"]
  G --> J["Ledger effect with non-claims"]
  H --> J
  I --> J
```

The most important object in the diagram is not the proof. It is the ledger
effect. A verifier pass may create a scoped update, not a broad promotion. A
failed artifact may become a durable failed-attempt record. A mismatch may
route to proof-adequacy review or tribunal review. A missing artifact may
block the requested tier. The system records what happened so the same
overclaim cannot be repeated under cleaner prose.

Different claims need different evidence. A finite invariant can be routed to
Lean. A source claim can require a citation dossier. A process claim can
require an executable procedure or replay log. A disputed governance claim can
require adversarial review. The common rule is that the justification type
must match the claim's risk, scope, and consumer requirement.

Spinoza's most useful behavior is downgrade discipline. Failed, timed-out, or
mismatched verification lowers confidence, blocks promotion, or escalates the
claim. It does not decorate the claim.

## Interfaces

The proof-carrying claim is the interface between ledger belief and verifier
artifacts.

A practical record needs at least these fields:

- `proof_claim_id`
- `claim_id`
- `claim_scope`
- `required_tier`
- `justification_type`
- `interpretation_mapping`
- `interpretation_confidence`
- `justification_artifact`
- `artifact_validity_state`
- `semantic_adequacy`
- `verifier`
- `verifier_result`
- `verifier_artifact_refs`
- `failed_attempt_refs`
- `formal_scope`
- `limitations`
- `consumer_requirements`
- `downgrade_rule`
- `claim_validity_effect`
- `residual_route`
- `tribunal_ref`
- `ledger_update`
- `source_refs`
- `support_state_effect`
- `non_claims`

These fields keep artifact validity separate from claim validity. A proof can
be valid for a narrow formal target while the natural-language claim receives
no promotion. A citation can be accurate while only supporting one part of a
claim. A replay log can prove that a run happened without proving that the
result generalizes. A tribunal dossier can document disagreement without
turning disagreement into consensus.

The claim ledger stores the resulting support effect. Lean and GenesisCode
provide formal or executable hooks where the claim is precise enough. UAT
receives claims whose interpretation, evidence conflict, or stakes need
role-separated review.

## Invariants

The requested tier is explicit.

A promoted claim needs a matching justification artifact.

Verifier failures and mismatches are non-promotional.

Artifact scope remains attached to the claim.

Those invariants are enough to stop the most common laundering path. A model
cannot say that a proof was attempted and therefore the claim is stronger. It
has to show the artifact, the verifier result, the interpretation mapping, the
formal scope, the limitations, the support-state effect, and the non-claims.

The finite Lean hooks support this record-level boundary. They model formal
tier reviews, failed-verifier promotion reviews, verifier results, claim
validity effects, and proof-carrying claim records. They prove that a valid
record carries the requested tier, interpretation mapping, confidence,
artifact references, formal scope, limitations, support-state effect, and
non-claims; that a passed verifier result requires verifier artifact
references; and that failed, timed-out, or mismatched verifier results require
non-promotional effects.

They do not prove that natural-language formalization is correct. They do not
prove theorem validity for arbitrary claims. They do not prove citation
accuracy, verifier quality, source interpretation, runtime behavior, or
epistemic correctness.

## Failure Modes

Certified delusion is the headline failure. The system has a formal-looking
object, but the object certifies the wrong claim.

Invalid formalization is the interpretation failure. The theorem or procedure
may be coherent, but the mapping from prose to artifact is wrong, too narrow,
or missing an important condition.

Justification absence is the tier failure. A claim is placed in a support
tier without the artifact that tier requires.

Verification theater is the social failure. The presence of a theorem tag,
proof label, citation count, or procedure log creates an aura of rigor while
the support boundary has not changed.

Stale proof reuse is the lifecycle failure. The claim later broadens, but the
old artifact is still invoked as if it covered the new scope.

Spinoza should make all five failures visible. If it cannot, the proof layer
is not protecting the book or the system.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable Spinoza slice is a tiered proof-carrying claim schema with
one non-philosophical invariant example and negative controls.

The repository already has part of that slice. The proof-carrying-claim
fixtures validate record shape for claim scope, justification type,
interpretation confidence, verifier artifact refs, failed-attempt refs, formal
scope, source refs, support-state effect, and non-claims. The local harness
checks three valid fixtures and five expected-invalid fixtures. It rejects
overpromotion paths such as a timeout that raises support, a mismatch that
promotes, a passed verifier missing artifact refs, or a negative result
without failed-attempt records.

This is useful, but narrow. It is a synthetic record-discipline harness. It
does not validate the referenced procedure, discharge a proof, check citation
truth, prove semantic equivalence, or run a deployed verifier.

The next minimum useful step would be a small real example: one formal target
that passes but supports only a narrower claim, one failed verifier result
that blocks promotion, one missing citation artifact that downgrades a
source-derived claim, and one contested interpretation routed to UAT.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature version is a justification compiler for high-value claims.

It would not try to formalize every sentence. It would decide which claims
deserve stronger treatment, choose the right justification type, record the
interpretation mapping, run the relevant verifier or review process, and write
the result back to the claim ledger without overpromoting the prose claim.

At product maturity, a Spinoza layer would include:

- tier assignment based on claim type, risk, scope, and requested support
  state;
- interpretation mappings from prose to formal targets, source propositions,
  procedure contracts, replay claims, benchmark claims, or tribunal questions;
- verifier integrations for Lean-like proof checks, schema checks, executable
  procedures, citation dossiers, replay logs, benchmark artifacts, and
  adversarial review;
- failed-attempt memory so bad mappings and unsupported promotions are not
  retried as if they were new;
- support-state effects that distinguish scoped update, no update, downgrade,
  block, and tribunal escalation;
- consumer requirements that state who is allowed to rely on the artifact and
  for what purpose.

The mature system would let rigor travel through the stack without inflating.
A proof could support a small invariant inside a planner, a replay log could
support one execution trace, a citation dossier could support one textual
claim, and a tribunal could preserve unresolved disagreement. Each artifact
would be strong because its boundary is explicit.

## What Is Proven, And What Is Not

The current repository proves and tests only record discipline.

The proof-carrying-claim harness passed locally for three valid fixtures and
five expected-invalid fixtures. The Lean module builds and proves finite
record predicates about artifact references, verifier results, and
non-promotional effects. Appendix C still keeps the chapter core claim at
`argument`.

The repo does not prove verifier quality, theorem validity for arbitrary
claims, citation accuracy, open-domain autoformalization, semantic equivalence
between prose and formal targets, source interpretation, runtime behavior, or
whole-system epistemic correctness. It does not reproduce Proof-Carrying Code,
import external Lean examples, or make GenesisCode operational.

The pending consolidation caveat also matters. This chapter is inside the
verification/adversarial-review package with Unified Adaptive Tribunal. This
reader pass does not execute, reject, or authorize that future merge. It
curates the current 54-chapter source chapter for the reader-manuscript cycle
only.

## Summary

Spinoza Verification and Proof-Carrying Claims is the stack's answer to
formal-looking overconfidence. It does not make every claim formal. It makes
selected claims carry the artifact, mapping, verifier result, limitation, and
ledger effect that would justify a stronger tier.

That makes proof useful in the way this book needs proof to be useful: narrow,
inspectable, reusable, and honest about what it does not cover. The next
chapter handles the cases where one verifier or one artifact is not enough.
Those cases need role-separated adversarial review.
